g.data-narration: Welcome to Transaction Concepts. Learn how databases ensure reliable data processing through ACID properties and concurrency control.
A transaction is a logical unit of work containing database operations that transforms the database from one consistent state to another.
g.text: "A collection of operations that performs a single logical function"

g.data-narration: Transactions ensure data integrity in multi-user environments, preventing inconsistencies from concurrent access and system failures.
ACID represents four fundamental properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability that every transaction must satisfy.
Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing execution: either all operations succeed together, or none are applied to the database.
g.text: Fund Transfer: Account A → Account B (₹1000)

g.data-narration: Consistency ensures valid state transitions, while Isolation hides intermediate results from concurrent transactions until commit.

g.text: DB must satisfy all constraints after transaction. No negative balances!
Concurrent T1, T2: T2 sees A=5000 (not intermediate 4000) until T1 commits

g.data-narration: Durability guarantees committed transaction effects persist permanently, surviving system crashes through non-volatile storage.
A transaction moves through five states: Active → Partially Committed → Committed, or Active → Failed → Aborted.
A schedule is a sequence of concurrent transaction operations. Serializability ensures correct interleaved execution equivalent to serial execution.
Conflict serializability is tested using precedence graphs. A schedule is serializable if its precedence graph is acyclic.
g.text: Two operations conflict if: Different transactions + At least one write + Same data item
Schedule S: T1 Read(X) → T2 Read(X) → T1 Write(X) → T2 Write(X)

g.data-narration: Two-Phase Locking has growing phase (acquire locks) and shrinking phase (release locks), ensuring serializability but may cause deadlocks.
g.text: T1 holds X, waits Y | T2 holds Y, waits X

g.data-narration: Three main concurrency control techniques: Lock-Based (2PL), Timestamp-Based (ordering), and Validation-Based (optimistic control).
Recovery techniques include log-based recovery (undo/redo), checkpoints for faster recovery, and shadow paging for atomic updates.
In fund transfer of ₹1000 from A(₹5000) to B(₹3000): atomicity ensures both operations succeed or both fail.
g.text: Atomic: Both succeed or both rollback | Consistent: Total₹8000 maintained

g.data-narration: Key points: ACID ensures reliability, transaction states track progress, serializable schedules are correct, 2PL ensures serializability.

g.text: States: Active → Partially Committed → Committed (or) Failed → Aborted
Serializable Schedule: Correct concurrent execution equivalent to serial
Conflict Serializability: Precedence graph must be acyclic
Recovery: UNDO uncommitted, REDO committed transactions

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Memorize ACID full forms, draw state diagram, practice precedence graphs, remember 2PL phases, know conflict operation conditions.
g.text: ✓ State Diagram: Active → Partial → Committed | Active → Failed → Aborted
All serial = serializable | Not vice versa!
